nativeRange.habitat: Slowly-flowing lowland rivers and river mainstream channels; found at or near the water surface in habitats with substrates of leaf litter, submerged woody structures, and sandy or gravelly bottoms

decorPreferences: large open swimming areas near the surface
river-biotope substrate of sand, fine gravel, and smooth water-worn rocks
driftwood and submerged branches replicating leaf-litter habitat
robust plants such as Microsorum or Anubias anchored to wood or rocks
strong water flow and high oxygenation via powerheads or spray bars
tight-fitting, escape-proof cover — essential; this species is a highly accomplished jumper

diet.notes: In the wild, the Silver Apollo Shark is primarily piscivorous, hunting fish fry, terrestrial and aquatic insects seized at the surface, and aquatic invertebrates. In captivity, it generally accepts frozen bloodworm, white mosquito larvae, vitamin-enriched brine shrimp, Mysis shrimp, krill, chopped prawn, river shrimp, and cockle. High-quality floating flake and slow-sinking pellets are also taken once the fish has acclimated. Offering live or frozen meaty foods several times per week helps maintain condition. Small fish and fry housed in the same aquarium are at risk of predation.

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species page image for Luciosoma setigerum; check current availability and license on the FishBase species page.

summary: The Silver Apollo Shark is a large, torpedo-shaped cyprinid from Southeast Asian rivers, commonly mistaken for a true shark. It reaches up to 26 cm, needs a school of at least six, and demands a very large, tightly covered aquarium with strong flow. Small fish and fry sharing its tank are at risk of predation.
careNotes: The Silver Apollo Shark is not a true shark but a sizable cyprinid from the rivers of Southeast Asia — Thailand, Laos, Cambodia, Vietnam, Malaysia, and Indonesia. Despite its common name, it belongs to the family Cyprinidae and is closely related to barbs and danios. A school of at least 6 is necessary; smaller groups lead to dominant individuals bullying subordinates. The tank must have a tightly fitting escape-proof cover — all Luciosoma species are highly accomplished jumpers and will leap at any gap. Filtration must be robust (turnover 4–5× tank volume per hour) and weekly water changes of 30–50% are considered routine to maintain the water quality and dissolved oxygen levels this active species demands. Water parameters should fall within pH 6.0–8.0, temperature 20–27°C, and soft to moderately hard water (gH 2–20). The Silver Apollo Shark is primarily a surface and mid-water swimmer; decor should prioritise wide open swimming lanes and the river-biotope aesthetic of sand, smooth gravel, driftwood, and attached hardy plants such as Anubias and Java fern. It will readily consume small fish, fry, and shrimp sharing its tank; suitable tankmates are large, robust cyprinids such as silver sharks, Barbonymus barbs, large Crossocheilus, botiid loaches, and giant danios. Avoid long-finned species, slow or docile fish, and anything small enough to swallow.
breedingNotes: No reliable captive breeding has been documented. The Silver Apollo Shark is presumed to be an open-water egg scatterer with no parental care, but the species has not been bred in the home aquarium. All aquarium specimens are wild-caught or commercially farm-produced. Breeding is currently considered not achievable in captivity.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Luciosoma setigerum
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (200–220 mm SL), temperature (20–26°C), pH (6.0–8.0), hardness (36–215 ppm), minimum tank dimensions (240 × 60 cm), native range (SE Asia: Thailand, Laos, Cambodia, Vietnam, Malaysia, Borneo, Sumatra, Java), habitat (slowly-flowing lowland rivers, leaf litter, woody structures), diet (terrestrial insects at surface, aquatic invertebrates, fish fry), and the critical note that all Luciosoma spp. are accomplished jumpers requiring tight-fitting covers.

additional.name: FishBase — Luciosoma setigerum
additional.notes: Cross-checked maximum size (26.0 cm SL), distribution (Malay Peninsula, Indochina, Indonesia), family (Danionidae/Cyprinidae depending on authority), order (Cypriniformes), habitat (river mainstream, low-gradient tributaries, surface of flowing waters), and diet (primarily piscivorous). FishBase maximum size of 26 cm used as ceiling for adultSizeCm range.

additional.name: Fishkeeper.co.uk — Apollo Shark (Luciosoma setigerum)
additional.notes: Supplementary UK trade source. Confirmed adult size (25 cm), temperature (22–27°C), pH (6.0–7.8), hardness (to 20 dH), minimum tank (6 ft × 2 ft × 2 ft), schooling requirement (6+), compatible genera (Barbonymus, Crossocheilus, Cyclocheilichthys, Puntius, Balantiocheilos, large botiid loaches), diet (flake, granules, frozen meaty foods), and breeding status (not bred in home aquarium).

additional.name: AquAnswers — Silver Apollo Shark Care Guide
additional.notes: Hobbyist-facing care guide. Confirmed escape risk (high — escape-proof lid required), lifespan (6–10 years), schooling minimum (4+, site recommends 6 for best welfare), 75-gallon minimum for small groups, and compatible species (giant danios, rainbowfish, silver dollars). Noted as secondary trade source — SeriouslyFish and FishBase preferred for scientific parameters.

fish.breedingNotes: No successful captive breeding has been documented. Presumed open-water egg scatterer with no parental care; all specimens in the trade are wild-caught or commercially farm-produced.
